exemplar.title:
In what ways does Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie develop 
the characters of Kambili and Amaka to challenge 
traditional gender roles in âPurple Hibiscusâ?

detailedExemplarData.title:
In what ways does Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie develop 
the characters of Kambili and Amaka to challenge 
traditional gender roles in âPurple Hibiscusâ?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Clear and focused communication of topic from title and introduction
Research question is narrow, explicitly stated, and maintained throughout
Organisation with section headings mirrors the inquiry and aids reader navigation
weaknesses: Occasional digressions into historical background that do not directly feed the central analysis
Methodology rationale and source-selection criteria are not explicitly articulated
Reliance on unevenly rigorous web sources weakens methodological coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay consistently communicates its focus on how Kambili and Amaka challenge gender roles, with a clearly stated and well-connected research question. Section headings and preview in the introduction establish purpose and guide the reader effectively. However, minor deviations into contextual background and an under-articulated methodologyâparticularly the uneven scholarly quality of some sources and lack of rationale for their selectionâlimit this criterion to the upper end of the 5-6 band. A more explicit methodological framework and tighter focus in background sections would be required for a perfect score.

strengths: Sound familiarity with the novelâs characters, plot moments, and symbols
Generally accurate use of literary terminology (symbolism, motif, foil) and feminist/conceptual language
Integration of secondary commentary to situate the novel within post-colonial and feminist debates
weaknesses: Some generalisations and repetitions dilute analytical precision
Inconsistent source robustness (e.g., reliance on Facebook posts, eNotes)
Minor imprecision in terminology (e.g., âWestern influencesâ vs. âWesternisationâ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good knowledge of Purple Hibiscus, effectively recounting key events and symbols and relating them to broader feminist and post-colonial frameworks. Literary terms and critical concepts are used accurately for the most part. However, occasional drift into summary, repetition, and less robust source choices prevent the work from reaching the top band. Consistently critical application of high-quality academic sources and tighter conceptual precision would strengthen this criterion.

strengths: Use of a dozen secondary sources directly relevant to the research question
Insightful symbolic interpretations (e.g., Kambiliâs stadium run as emotional emancipation)
Recognition of multiple modalities of resistance through vocal and internal forms
weaknesses: Analysis often remains surface-level, paraphrasing rather than dissecting textual language
Evaluation is one-sided with limited engagement in counter-arguments or scholarly debate
Dependence on tertiary sources (Litcharts, homework.study.com) weakens critical depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay conducts adequate, mostly relevant research and moves beyond summary to interpret characterisation and symbolism in service of the research question. Several strengthsâsuch as nuanced contrasts between characters and insightful symbolic readingsâdemonstrate critical engagement. However, the analysis frequently stops short of a deep linguistic unpacking of quotations, and evaluative discussion lacks sustained counter-argument or engagement with scholarly perspectives. These factors place the work solidly in the upper half of the 4-6 band, corresponding to a score of 8/12.

strengths: Clear overall structure with title page, table of contents, labelled sections, conclusion, and bibliography
Section headings mirror the research focus and enhance navigation
Consistent logical progression from character analysis to thematic synthesis
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks page numbers, hindering quick reference
Inconsistent citation formatting and broken URLs diminish polish
Frequent grammatical and phrasing lapses distract at points
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is strong in its adherence to expected Extended Essay structure: all required elements are present, and headings guide the reader clearly through the argument. Minor formatting lapsesâmissing page numbers in the table of contents, inconsistent citation style, and occasional language errorsâprevent a flawless execution. These minor but noticeable issues limit this criterion to the top of the 1-2 band, yielding 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Methodology completeness and effectiveness** â For a Category 1 essay the central âmethodâ is close textual analysis supported by secondary criticism.  The student cites a mixture of sources (academic articles, ResearchGate PDFs, blog posts, Litcharts, Facebook pages).  Citations are uneven in scholarly rigour and some web links are incomplete, indicating only partial evidence of informed selection.  The range, while adequate, is not extensive; primary evidence relies heavily on paraphrase with few embedded quotations.

The organisational method (section headings echoing the table of contents) is clear, but a rationale for source selection or critical framework is not articulated.  Hence the work is âmostly completeâ rather than fully effective, placing it in the 3-4 band at the upper end.

**Layout and formatting** â Essential EE formatting elements are present: title, word count, page numbering (though not always in the footer), section headings, and a bibliographic list.  Citations generally follow a recognisable style, albeit inconsistently (variable capitalisation, broken URLs).  Spelling and punctuation lapses are frequent but do not impede comprehension.

Because layout conventions are mostly but not flawlessly applied, the strand fits the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 reflects the noticeable but non-fatal inconsistencies.

**Research quality and relevance** â Roughly a dozen secondary sources are consulted, most of which deal directly with Purple Hibiscus or gender in Nigerian fiction, so relevance is evident.  Nevertheless, several sources are popular or tertiary (Litcharts, Sunflower Bookshelf, Facebook blog), diluting academic quality.  Citations are sometimes incomplete, and there is little explicit critical dialogue with these sources.

Because the research is adequate and mostly pertinent but not consistently scholarly, the mid-band (4-6) is appropriate; the work sits centrally at 5.

**Topic communication and clarity** â The student establishes the focus from the title and first paragraph, explicitly identifying that the essay will analyse how Kambili and Amaka are used to challenge gender roles in Purple Hibiscus.  The introduction sets out why this focus matters (patriarchy in post-colonial Nigeria) and previews the four organising sections, so the reader knows exactly what is being investigated and why.

Minor lapses occur (occasional digressions into historical background and definition of symbolism that do not always feed the line of inquiry), yet the overarching purpose remains clear and is consistently returned to.  The level of clarity and audience awareness therefore fulfils the 5-6 band, albeit not quite with the polish that would merit a 6.

**Discussion and evaluation** â An argument line is maintained (both characters disrupt gender norms), but evaluative momentsâsuch as weighing the effectiveness of different forms of resistance or critiquing scholarsâare brief.  Counter-arguments or limitations (e.g., the novelâs open ending, complexity of Western relocation) are acknowledged but not explored in depth.

Because the discussion shows some critical engagement yet remains largely one-sided and repetitive, it meets the lower end of âgood argument development with partially critical evaluation,â warranting 4 within the 4-6 band.

**Analysis depth and effectiveness** â The student moves beyond summary to interpret how character contrasts, narrative voice and symbolism convey resistance to patriarchy.  Paragraphs generally relate evidence to the research question, and some quotations are analysed for language effects (e.g., Amakaâs rejection of English names).

Yet the analysis often remains surface-level, paraphrasing rather than dissecting textual detail, and conclusions occasionally restated rather than built from evidence.  Support therefore is âpartiallyâ rather than fully persuasive, positioning the essay in the middle of the 4-6 band.

**Structure appropriateness** â The essay follows conventional EE structure: title page with required details, table of contents, labelled sections, conclusion and bibliography.  Headings mirror the research focus, aiding navigation, and there is logical progression from individual character discussion to comparative and thematic synthesis.

Minor structural issues exist (some sections contain tangential background; page-numbered fragments break flow), yet overall it âclearly meetsâ structural requirements.  Hence the top band (3-4) with 4 is justified.

**Knowledge of topic and sources** â The student demonstrates sound familiarity with Purple Hibiscus, accurately recounting characters, plot moments and symbols (e.g., yam-peeling episode, Father Amadiâs run, purple hibiscus motif).  Secondary commentary is integrated to situate the novel within post-colonial and feminist debates, showing clearâthough not exhaustiveâunderstanding.

However, analysis sometimes drifts into generalisation or repetition, and source use is not always critical (e.g., descriptive borrowing from eNotes).  The knowledge is therefore âgoodâ rather than âexcellent,â meriting the upper-middle band.

**Use of terminology and concepts** â The essay refers to literary devices such as symbolism, juxtaposition, motif, foil and narration, and employs concepts like patriarchy, post-colonialism and feminism with mostly correct usage.  Terminology is generally integrated into sentences rather than itemised.

Occasional slips in phrasing (âconformation name,â âlaments on how,â âWestern influencesâ when meaning âWesternisationâ) reveal some imprecision, preventing full consistency.  Overall usage aligns with the 3-4 band at the higher end.

**Research question focus and appropriateness** â The question is stated verbatim on the title page and is narrow enough for detailed literary analysis (âIn what ways does Adichie develop Kambili and Amaka to challenge traditional gender roles â¦?â).  Throughout the body, each section is explicitly linked back to this question: Kambiliâs transformation, Amakaâs catalytic role, and their joint defiance of patriarchy.  The tight pairing of characterisation and gender norms demonstrates a focused and appropriate avenue of inquiry.

Occasional digressions into plot summary or socio-political background marginally dilute the focus but do not undermine it; overall the essay stays effectively connected to the stated line of investigation, warranting the 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and focused, directly framing the inquiry into how Adichie develops Kambili and Amaka to challenge gender roles.
The word count is included prominently on the title page, meeting the requirement and ensuring transparency about the essayâs length.
The title âThe Role of Characterization in Exploring Identity and Resistanceâ effectively conveys the topic but could be more specific by mentioning the novel or characters analyzed.
The absence of page numbers in the table of contents limits reader navigation; adding corresponding page references would enhance usability.
Table of Contents entries lack page numbers, reducing structural clarity and hindering quick reference for the reader.

While providing useful context, the extended historical background on the Igbo people and patriarchy occupies space that could be more effectively spent directly introducing the charactersâ gender role analysis.
The explicit roadmap outlining the essayâs four key sections enhances clarity and guides the reader through the argumentâs progression.
The final sentence introduces a new point on gender equality without sufficient development; trimming or expanding it would ensure a cohesive closure.
The bibliography entries show inconsistent citation style (mix of APA and other formats) and broken URLs, undermining academic rigor.
Standardize citation formatting (e.g., APA or MLA) and ensure all online sources include access dates and stable URLs.
The analysis of the title symbol relies heavily on a tertiary source (homework.study.com) without critical interrogation, weakening the scholarly depth of interpretation.
Consider streamlining the setting description by focusing on details that directly impact character interactions, tying the socio-political context back to gender dynamics.
The description of setting is accurate but reiterates earlier contextual information; tighter integration with subsequent character analysis would improve flow.
The citation to a Facebook post for Kambiliâs early characterization is not academically robust; replacing with peer-reviewed criticism or direct novel quotations would strengthen the argument.
To deepen analysis, integrate a key quotation from the novel and unpack the specific language choices that reveal Kambiliâs internal struggle.
The description of Kambiliâs exposure to Nsukka effectively highlights the environmental catalyst for her transformation, demonstrating clear understanding of narrative development.
The quote on Amaka refusing an English name is presented without commentary on its literary function; expanding on its thematic significance would reinforce critical analysis.
The symbolic interpretation of Kambili running at the stadium is insightful, linking physical liberation to emotional emancipation.
Explore the narrative voice shift more precisely by examining changes in sentence structure or diction between early and later chapters.
This concluding sentence restates previous points without synthesizing new insights or addressing potential counterarguments in the novelâs portrayal of defiance.
The overview of Amakaâs cultural stance versus Catholic upbringing clearly contextualizes her role as a counterpoint to Kambili.
The discussion on Amakaâs relationship with Papa-Nnukwu repeats earlier points and could be condensed to maintain analytical focus.
Integrate analysis of the quotationâs tone and imagery by exploring how the colloquial language reflects Amakaâs irreverence.
The sentence about Beatrice is underdeveloped and introduces a new comparative thread without sufficient textual evidence or elaboration.
The reflection on Amakaâs relocation to America adds nuance by acknowledging character complexity and unintended ironies in her resistance.
Consider evaluating how secondary sources address this relocation to broaden the critical conversation and support your interpretation.
The paragraph summarizes rather than critically engages with feminist theory; linking to specific feminist frameworks would deepen the analysis.
The identification of literary devices like symbolism and juxtaposition is precise and reinforces the thematic argument about varied forms of resistance.
To strengthen evaluative depth, contrast your discussion of Kambiliâs symbolic pain with a scholarly perspective from your bibliography.
This paragraph conflates thematic analysis with narrative summary; separating interpretive claims from plot description would improve clarity.
The extension of the purple hibiscus metaphor to the charactersâ resistance is well-articulated and underscores the essayâs central thesis.
The engagement with feminist themes shows conceptual understanding and aligns the charactersâ experiences with broader gendered power structures.

Several sentences in this section suffer from grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g., âKambili starts as a silent and submissive character but he gradually starts to find her voiceâ), which distracts from the argument.
The distinction between vocal and internal forms of resistance is nuanced and emphasizes that feminist resistance manifests in multiple modalities.
Introduce a counterpoint by considering how the novelâs ambiguous ending complicates the notion of lasting female empowerment.
The conclusion reiterates earlier summaries but lacks a final evaluative insight or reflection on the research questionâs implications for literary scholarship.
Consider ending with a statement that situates your findings within contemporary feminist discourse to underline the essayâs academic contribution.
